title: Personal Data Protection Code of Practice (Utilities — Electricity) 2022
summary: This sector-specific Code applies to electricity utility providers in the Utilities class (Class 11 of the Class of Data Users Order 2013), including Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB), Sarawak Energy Berhad, and Sabah Electricity Sdn Bhd. It supplements the General Code 2022 with requirements tailored to the electricity sector, particularly addressing the significant privacy implications of smart meter data, which can reveal detailed patterns of household occupancy, behaviour, and appliance usage. The Code takes into account requirements under the Electricity Supply Act 1990 and Energy Commission (Suruhanjaya Tenaga) regulations.

provisions.title: Scope and Application
provisions.content: This Code applies to licensed electricity supply companies and their subsidiaries engaged in the supply, distribution, and metering of electricity in Malaysia. It addresses the processing of consumer personal data including account information, consumption data from conventional and smart meters, billing records, and data generated by demand response programmes.
requirements: Electricity utilities must map all personal data processing activities in the ROPA
Smart meter data must be classified separately as high-sensitivity consumption data
This Code must be read together with the General Code 2022 and the Water Utilities Code where an entity operates in both sectors

provisions.title: Smart Meter Data
provisions.content: Smart meters collect highly granular electricity consumption data (interval data at 15 or 30-minute intervals) that can reveal whether a household is occupied, daily routines, the types of appliances used, and household composition. This data must be treated as high-sensitivity personal data. Electricity utilities must: (a) provide consumers with specific notice of smart meter data collection at installation; (b) use smart meter interval data only for billing, grid management, and fault detection without additional consent; (c) obtain explicit consent for any secondary uses of interval data, including energy efficiency programmes, third-party data sharing, demand response schemes, and research; (d) aggregate interval data before sharing it with third parties (minimum 15-minute intervals, anonymized where possible); and (e) retain interval data for no longer than 13 months for billing purposes without specific consent for longer retention.
requirements: Smart meter notice must be provided to consumers at or before meter installation
Interval data secondary use requires specific consent
Aggregated or anonymized data may be shared without consent where re-identification is not possible
13-month maximum retention for interval data without consent

provisions.title: Consumer Account Data
provisions.content: Consumer account data (name, NRIC, address, contact details, bank account for direct debit) must be: (a) collected only for the purposes of providing electricity supply and related services; (b) not used for marketing of third-party products without consent; (c) updated when consumers notify changes; (d) disclosed to the Energy Commission and other regulators only for regulatory purposes; and (e) retained for the duration of the consumer relationship plus 7 years for regulatory and billing dispute purposes.
requirements: Consumer account data must not be used for third-party marketing without consent
Account data accuracy must be maintained — utilities must have a process for accepting consumer address/contact updates
Energy Commission disclosures are mandatory and lawful under the Electricity Supply Act

provisions.title: Debt Management and Collection
provisions.content: Where electricity utilities engage in debt collection or credit reporting for unpaid accounts, they must: (a) ensure that credit bureau reporting is disclosed in the consumer privacy notice; (b) report only accurate and current debt information; (c) provide consumers with notice before reporting defaults to credit bureaus; (d) correct credit bureau reports promptly upon payment or dispute resolution; and (e) use debt collection agents only under data processing agreements limiting use of consumer data to the debt recovery purpose.
requirements: Credit bureau reporting disclosure in consumer notice is mandatory
Pre-reporting notice to consumer is recommended
Debt collectors must operate under DPAs
Correction of credit bureau reports post-settlement must be made within 14 days

provisions.title: Security of Consumer Data Systems
provisions.content: Electricity utilities must secure consumer data systems including advanced metering infrastructure (AMI) and metering data management systems (MDMS) with: (a) network segmentation isolating AMI and MDMS from corporate and internet networks; (b) encrypted communication between smart meters and the utility's head-end system; (c) access controls and MFA for MDMS access; (d) comprehensive audit logging for access to and export of consumption data; (e) regular security assessments of AMI and MDMS infrastructure; and (f) an incident response plan covering scenarios including smart meter data breaches and AMI network attacks.
requirements: AMI networks must be on isolated network segments
Smart meter communication encryption is required
MDMS access must require MFA for all users
Security assessments of critical infrastructure at least annually
